titlestmt.subtitle: Papers of William A. Little, 1820-2013, at the Lilly Library, Indiana University
titlestmt.author: Sarah Helen Carter

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday April 8, 2026 at 13:57

did.abstract: The Little, William mss. consists of organ literature, personal correspondence, music manuscripts and published music from the personal collection of William A. Little.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: William A. Little [William Alfred Little, IV] (b. July 28, 1929, in Boston, Suffolk County, Massachusetts; d. November 26, 2019 in Charlottesville City, Virginia) was a musicologist, organist, and educator. He earned a Bachelor of Arts in German and French from Tufts University (1951), a Licentiate of Trinity College London (LTCL) in Organ Performance (1952), and a Master of Arts in German from Harvard University (1953). After completing two years of military service as a Corporal in the US Army in Germany (1953–1955), he completed a Doctor of Philosophy in German at the University of Michigan (1961).

Dr. Little’s career as Professor of German began at Williams College (1957–1963). Following a brief tenure at Tufts University (1963–1966), where he served as Associate Professor and Chair of the German Department. In 1966, he accepted a position at the University of Virginia as Professor of German and Chair of the University’s newly established German Department. He remained at UVA for the duration of his teaching career, retiring in 1996 as Professor Emeritus of German and Music.

Dr. Little’s dissertation research concerned the Austrian dramatist Franz Grillparzer (1791–1872), and his further scholarship in German focused on 18th- and 19th-century Austrian literature. His monograph on the poet Gottfried August Bürger was published by Twayne Publishers in 1974. He was active in several professional associations related to German literature and language education, which included serving on the National Executive Council of the American Association of Teachers of German (1968–1978) and as editor of The German Quarterly (the official journal of the American Association of Teachers of German) (1970–1978).

Dr. Little was a skilled organist, having trained in Boston under George H. Faxon (1947– 1953) and Carl Weinrich (1953) and in Ann Arbor with Robert Noehren (1955–1957). He also studied composition with Everett Titcomb (1949–1953). While completing his baccalaureate at Tufts University, he served as Organist of the College (1947–1951) and performed as organist at several area churches. Further, while teaching at Williams College, he served as College Organist (1959–1963), and, after relocating to Virginia, he was the Organist and Choirmaster at St. Paul’s Church, Ivy (Episcopal) (1966–1986).

By the late 1970s, Dr. Little’s research efforts had largely shifted from German literature to musicology, with a particular focus on the life and works of Felix Mendelssohn Bartholdy. In 1986, he discovered Mendelssohn’s lost organ works in the Biblioteka Jagiellskonska in Kraków, Poland; the autograph scores had been moved from Berlin after the end of WWII, and suspicions were that they had been lost or destroyed in transit. Following this re-discovery, Dr. Little edited and published Mendelssohn’s Complete Organ Works in five volumes (London: Novello, 1987– 1990) and wrote a companion monograph to accompany the edition, titled Mendelssohn and the Organ (New York: Oxford, 2010). Dr. Little was a member of several musicological associations, including the American Bach Society, American Brahms Society, and Neue Bachgesellschaft (Leipzig), and in spring 1996, he taught at the Eastman School of Music as Visiting Professor of Musicology. He was also a long-time and active member of the American Guild of Organists (AGO): he was among the founding members of the AGO’s Charlottesville/Albemarle chapter, served as the chapter’s dean for three terms, participated on the on national AGO Committee for Continuing Education, and contributed several articles to the organization’s journal, The American Organist.
Dr. Little died on November 29, 2019, at his home in Charlottesville, VA. He was survived by his beloved ex-wife, Mary “Pidge” Schimmel Cash.
Biography from:

Lowther, Gail E., Veronica Siebert, and Nathan Savant. “Wm. A. Little Collection [Finding Aid].” Eastman School of Music, University of Rochester, 2023. https://www.esm.rochester.edu/sibley/files/Wm-A-Little-Collection.pdf.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.
Many collections are housed offsite; retrieval requires advance notice. Please make an appointment a minimum of one week in advance of your visit.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Photography and digitization may be restricted for some collections. Copyright restrictions may apply. Before publishing, researchers are responsible for securing permission from all applicable rights holders, then filling out the Permission to Publish form.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Processed by Sarah Helen Carter. Completed in 2025.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: [Item], Little, William, mss., Lilly Library, Indiana University, Bloomington, Indiana.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: See the Wm. A. Little Collection, Sibley Music Library (Ruth T. Watanabe Special Collections), Eastman School of Music at the University of Rochester.

Finding aid: Lowther, Gail E., Veronica Siebert, and Nathan Savant. “Wm. A. Little Collection [Finding Aid].” Eastman School of Music, University of Rochester, 2023. https://www.esm.rochester.edu/sibley/files/Wm-A-Little-Collection.pdf.

did.unittitle: American Guild of Organists Pasadena Chapter Year Book
The American Guild of Organists. A Twenty-year Subject/Author Index...

odd.head: General
odd.p: Compiled by David Fienen, Gustavus Adolphus College

odd.head: Full Title
odd.p: The American Guild of Organists. A Twenty-year Subject/Author Index of Music (1967-1968), Music and RCCO (1968-1978) and The American Organist (1979-1987)

did.unittitle: The American Guild of Organists. Index (October 1967-September 1977)
De Graaf. Literatuur over het orgel / Literature on the organ

did.physdesc: 2 copies

did.unittitle: Kuntze, C., ed. Die Orgel und ihr Bau
did.physdesc: Leipzig: F.E.C. Leuckart, 1875.. Dritte gänzlicgh umgearbeitete Auflage von. Joh. Julius Seidel's gleichnamigem Werke. Disbound. Worn. 177 pp.

did.unittitle: Locher, Carl. Die Orgel-Register und ihre Klangfarben ... Dritte, stark vermerhte Auflage.
did.physdesc: Bern: Emil Baumgart, 1904. Disbound, Worn. 141 pp.
Photocopy of the Erfurt 1867 edition

odd.head: Full Title
odd.p: Musica Sacra: Vollständiges Verzeichniss aller seit dem Jahre 1750-1867 gedruckt ershienener, Composition für die Orgel, Lehrbücher für die Orgel, Schriften über Orgelbaukunst

did.unittitle: Ramin. Gedanken zur Klärung des Orgelproblems
Satorius. Bibliography of Concerts for Organ and Orchestra
did.physdesc: Cover stamped and signed by Douglas L. Butler
Heavily degraded, cover and half title page detached

did.unittitle: The Tracker Journal of the Organ Historical Society, Index to Volumes 1-33
Publications on Organ Composers and Performers
J.S. Bach Tercentenary [Program], Royal Albert Hall
Guilmant, Alexandre. Organ Recitals, Festival Hall, World's Fair, St. Louis [facsimilie]

odd.head: General
odd.p: "Published in facsimilie in 1985 by The Organ Historical Society"

odd.head: General
odd.p: "Published as a special issue of the American Choral Review, vol. 20, no. 2"

did.unittitle: Scull, Colin S. [comp]. A list of the masters of the children, organists and composers of the English Chapel Royal from 1444 to the present day
Publications on Specific Organs and Organ Builders
did.physdesc: A collection of approximately 90 pamphlets and miscellaneous ephemera relating to specific organs and organ builders. ca.1920-1990.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Bon Air, Virginia: St. Michael's Episcopal Church Boston, Massachusetts: Cathedral Church of St. Pau! Boston: First Church of Christ, Scientist Boston: Greek Orthodox Cathedral Chapel Hill, North Carolina: Duke University Memorial Chapel Deland, Florida: Elizabeth Hall, Stetson University Evansville, Indiana: The Methodist Temple Madison, Virginia: Hebron Lutheran Church Methuen, Massachusetts: Methuen Memorial Hall New Haven, Connecticut:" Trinity Church New York: The Cathedral of Saint John the Divine Philadelphia Wanamaker Organ San Francisco: California Palace of the Legion of Honor Wellesley, Massachusetts: The Wellesley College Sweelinck Organ Williamsburg, Virginia: Bruton Parish Church Wilmington, Delaware: First Unitarian Church Yale Institute of Sacred Music, 1997 Organ Competition

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Bigelow; Fiske; Noack

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Bath Abbey Lambeth Parish Saint Michael, Coventry Selby Abbey York Minster

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Strasbourg: Eglise Reformée Saint-Paul

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Aachen, Basilika Vierzehnheiligen, Frauenzell, Justinuskirche, Lübeck, Marienkirche, Neustift bei Brixen, Obermarchtal, Ottobeuren, Paderborn, Passauer, Pfarrkirche Gartsten, Rot an der Rot, Rötha, Schwäbisch Gmünd, St. Augustin, St. Mihiel, Steinhausen, Toul, Weingarten, Weissenau, Wiblingen, Wittenberg, Zwiefalten
Includes a group of 27 original small photographs (10 x 7 cms.) of organs in various German towns, 1954. Each identified in manuscript on verso

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Beckerath Heissler Rieger Trost Walcker Weigle

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Fischingen; Ladesgast-Orgel im Dom zu Scherin

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Genève

did.physdesc: Small archive of 21 typed letters signed, dating from 1950 to 1954, from the German organist and composer Max Drischner to Little, together with miscellaneous ephemera. Most letters densely written, almost all signed in full (very occasionally with initials) in purple or dark pink pencil, many with handstamp "Max Drischner Kirchenmusikdirektor, Cembalist, Kantor und Organist an St. Nicolai in Brieg (Schles.) jetzt Herrenberg, Württ. (14a) Kirchgasse I1" and with his return address stamp, label, or printed address. In German. With significant musical content including discussion of Drischner's compositions, publications, musical activities, etc.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: A German organist and composer, Drischner studied the harpsichord with Landowska and the organ with Schweitzer. "In the early 1920s he was active in concerts devoted to the revival of old music. Drischner's main position, from 1924 until the end of World War II, was at St Nicholas in Brieg (now Brzeg), where he was organist and Kantor. The Engler organ there was restored under his direction from 1926 to 1928. Afler the war he lived in Erfurt, Herrenberg and finally settled in Goslar in 1955. His published compositions are mainly sacred choral and organ music. The organ music in particular is based on older models, especially in the use of chorales. Much of the published sacred vocal music is intentionally simple; often the accompaniment may be played on the harmonium or piano in place ofthe organ." William D. Gudger, revised by Klaus Kirchberg in Grove Music Online.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 8 September 1948. 20 pp. To "Wilhelm" [possibly Little]. With handwritten list.
27 March 1950. 2 pp. Oblong octavo. Inscribed. With typed envelope addressed to Little in Somerville, Massachusetts
17 April 1950. 1 p. Narrow oblong octavo. A brief note, signed by Grete Drischner. Envelope lacking
1 June 1950. 2 pp. Oblong octavo. One line underlined, inscribed. With typed envelope addressed to Little in Somerville
1 June 1950. 2 pp. One line underlined. With carbon copy of letter from Albert Schweitzer to Drischner dated Lambarene 12 March 1950.
20 June 1950. 2 pp. Oblong octavo. With typed envelope addressed to Little in Somerville
29 June 1950. 2 pp. Oblong octavo. With typed envelope addressed to Little in Somerville
3 August 1950. 2 pp. Oblong octavo. With typed envelope addressed to Little in Somerville
31 August 1950. 1 p. Folio. With carbon copies of Drischner's letters of 17 and 19 August 1950 to editor Walter Schulz. 1-1/4 pp.
1 October 1951. 1 p. Oblong octavo. With publisher's advertisement for Drischner's "Die Weihnachtsgeschichte." With typed envelope addressed to Little in Somerville
12 November 1951. 1 p. Oblong octavo. With typed enveloped addressed to Little in Somerville
10 December 1951. 1 p. Oblong octavo. To Little in Somerville
25 February 1953. 2 pp. Quarto. To Little in Somerville

17 April 1953. 1 p. Oblong octavo. To Little in Somerville. Together with a 1-1/2 p. letter to Drischner from Little's mother dated 4 October 1953 in English. With postcard photograph of Albert Schweitzer
22 October 1953. 1-1/2 pp. Folio. with publisher's advertisement for Drischner's Die Weihnachtsgeschichte. With typed envelope addressed to Little in Bismarck-Kaserne
22 October 1953. 1 p. aerogram. Folio, Addressed to Little's mother in Somerville
6 November 1953. 1 p. Oblong octavo. With postal card of Herrenberg. With typed envelope to Little in Bismarck-Kaserne.
8 November 1953. 1 p. aerogram. Addressed to Mrs. Little in Somerville
14 November 1953. 2 pp. Oblong octavo. With typed envelope addressed to Little in Bismarck Kaserne. With invitation to Drischer's organ concert, Marktkirche zu Goslar, 22 November
16 December 1953. 1 p. Oblong octavo. With typed envelope addressed to Little in Bismarck Kaserne with forwarding instructions

December 1953. Printed Christmas letter. With one correction in pencil, a paragraph marked in red pencil, 2 lines underlined in red pencil, and a short note typed inviting Little to come to a celebration on Christmas evening at 5 p.m. With typed envelope addressed to Little in Patch Barracks, Stuttgart Vaihingen

8 August 1954. 1 p. On a postal card. With accompanying note on letterhead of Die Gesellschaft der Orgelfreunde and contemporary newspaper clipping with photograph, With typed envelope addressed to Little in Patch Barracks, Stuttgart Vaihingen.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 2 small original photographs of Albert Schweitzer and Drischner, 60 x 60 mm.
3 postcard photographs: one of Drischner's house in Herrenberg and 2 views of the town

did.unittitle: Ein Orgelmeister der Gegenwart Max Drischner - ein Sonderfall der Neuen Musik aus "Die Union," Landeszeitung Sachsen der CDU, Dresden, Nr. 118
did.physdesc: Inscribed and signed in dark pink ink with initials. With typed envelope addressed to Little at Harvard.

Small archive of material relating to the virtuoso German organist Fritz Heitmann containing a biographical statement in Heitmann's autograph, letters, programs, a published biography, and associated documents.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Fritz Heitmann (1891-1953) studied with Karl Straube, Josef Pembaur (a pupil of Liszt) and Reger (composition). "... in 1925 he was elected professor of the Akademie für Kirchen- und Schulmusik in Berlin. He became organist of Berlin Cathedral in 1930 ... He exerted a considerable influence in the first half ofthe century through his many pupils, and by his enthusiasm for neglected music, particularly that of Reger. A brilliant virtuoso, he made mumerous recordings, broadcasts and international concert tours." Gerhard Wienke in Grove Music Online.

did.physdesc: Addressed to Professor Adolph Strube, editor in chief of Der Kirchenmusiker. According to a manuscript note at the foot of the page by Professor Little, this manuscript account was written by Heitmann in the All Souls Room of the Church of the Advent in Boston in 1953, on the occasion of his last visit to Boston.

odd.head: General
odd.p: In Heitmann's hand with biographical details

odd.head: General
odd.p: Includes article by Little entitled "Fritz Heitmann in den Vereinigten Staaten."

did.physdesc: 1 page of a "Lustpostbrief." To Little, thanking him for his letter and article.
Thanking Little for sending the manuscript of his article on Heitmann. With envelope.

did.unittitle: The First Church of Christ, Scientist, Boston
The Church of The Advent, Boston
Printed notice in appreciation of condolences
did.physdesc: Sent on the occasion of Heitmann's death, with manuscript note to lower margin, most likely in the hand of Heitmann's wife. With envelope

did.unittitle: Voge, Richard. "Fritz Heitmann: Das Leben eines Deutschen Organisten in verbindung mit Elisabeth Heitmann dargestellt von Richard Voge"
did.physdesc: Octavo. Original dark ivory linen-backed publisher's olive green boards. 1f. (recto half-title, verso tipped-in bust-length frontispiece portrait of Heitmann), 1f. (recto title, verso copyright), 1f. (contents), 7-166, 1f. (recto index, verso blank).

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: This is the primary published biographical account of Heitmann.

did.physdesc: Small archive consisting of a short musical manuscript (possibly autograph); letters from the composer's wife Theodora to Little; a small group of printed editions of music for organ; and associated ephemera relating to the Munich composer and teacher.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Gottfried Rüdinger (1886-1946) "studied composition with Max Reger in Leipzig and church music under Wilhelm Widmann at Eichstätt Cathedral. From 1910 Rüdinger taught in Munich, first privately and thenfrom 1920 as a member of the theory staff at the Academy of Music, where he became professor in 1938 and continued to teach until his death. From 1916 he was also a conductor of church choirs in Berg am Laim. Much of Rüdinger's music, such as the Romantische Serenade op. 9, shows the influence of Brahms and Reger; his vocal works make use of folksongs. Of his approximately 150 compositions, the last third (from the war years) remain unpublished." William D. Gudger in Grove Music Online.

did.unittitle: 3 Madrigale für vierstimigen gemischten Chor. 2.) Ave Maria
did.physdesc: Manuscript (possibly autograph). 1f. (recto collective title. verso blank), 3 pp. Folio. Notated in ink on 14-stave music paper on rectos only. Slightly worn, foxed, and creased. With small handstamp ("G. Rüdinger") in dark purple ink to upper outer corner of title. In envelope addressed to Little in Stuttgart Vaibingen.
Signed. 4 pp. Folio. In pencil. Densely written. With autograph envelope addressed to Little in Somerville, Massachusetts
Signed. 1-1/3 pp.
Addressed to Little in Somerville
Addressed to Little in Somerville

did.unittitle: [Op. 4]. Sonate in g-moll für Orgel... Opus 4
did.physdesc: Light green wrappers with titling within border. [1] (title), 2-24 pp. With signature of William A. Little to upper outer corner of upper wrapper. Wrappers detached. Worn and browned; some leaves detached.
Oblong quarto. Dark ivory wrappers printed in dark brown. 1f. (recto title printed in dark brown, verso contents) 3-16 pp. With annotations in pencil and ink. Slightly worn and soiled.
Oblong quarto. Dark ivory wrappers printed in dark purple. [1] (title printed in dark purple), 2-15, [i] (blank) pp. With signature of William A. Little to upper outer corner of title.

did.unittitle: Präludium und Doppelfuge für Orgel ... Preis M 2,25
did.physdesc: Light blue printed wrappers. [1] (title), 2-13, [i] (blank) pp. With signature of William A. Little to upper outer corner of upper wrapper. Wrappers worn and detached. Minor internal wear and creasing; some leaves detached.

did.unittitle: Ein Münchner Komponist Gottfried Rüdiinger, Münchner Abendbaltt, 1. XII
Typed Notes on Rüdinger's Life and Works
Berrsche, Alexander. "Gottfried Rüdinger op. 68 sonate in h-moll für Orgel"
did.physdesc: 1 p. Oblong octavo.

did.unittitle: Junk, Victor. "Orgelsonate h-moll op. 68, Elegie für Violine und Orgel op. 77"
did.physdesc: 3/4 p. Folio.

odd.head: General
odd.p: From the Zeitschrift für Musik Hefł 7, Juli 1931.

did.physdesc: 1-1/4 p. Folio.
Two blank photograph postcards of Gottfried Rüdinger
Group of 13 early 19th century organ-related autograph documents dating from the year the Cathedral was built, 1820, to 1842.
All documents slightly worn; some creased at folds; other minor defects as noted. In very good condition overall.

Introductory note in Little's hand on his personal red-bordered ivory letterhead with "Kirklea, Ivy, Virginia" at head: "These documents were given to me by Dean Van Etten in the spring of 1953, before I entered the army [signed] W. A. Little, See final paragraph of the brochure, The Story of Music. Dean van Etten told me to take some mementos of the attic boxes in appreciation for arranging, sorting and documenting."

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: The "Dean van Etten" referenced in the note is Rev. Dr. Edwin Jan Van Etten (1884-1956), dean emeritus of St. Paul's Protestant Episcopal Cathedral.

did.unittitle: Holiday card signed Edwin J van Etten
did.physdesc: Holiday card signed Edwin J van Etten with envelope address to Little
Pledging to pay Wilby $50, signed by Goodrich

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: William Mercellus Goodrich (1777-1833), an important American organ builder and founder of an organ building business in Boston, built the organ at St. Paul's.

did.physdesc: 1/2 page, "To Henry Codman of the Yankee Nobility." With curious text: "In this land of Liberty & Equality each & every man also any body of men have rights to do their will on this ground ofthe St. Paul pile of stone stands fair & firm, but ifthe spirrit[!] of 76 had not become extinct, such a downright monarchical, aristocratical, bastile[!]-looking prayer warehouse, would not outrage the feelings of republicans, in truth of spirrit[!], 24 hours longer. I [illegible] who belongs to no Sect or Party." One fold partially split; tear with resultant hole at seal with loss of signature. With integral address panel with note in another hand to upper margin: "Anonymous letter recd. on the morning after the Consecration."

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: The "Henry Codman" referred to is possibly the Harvard-educated (graduated in 1808) Boston lawyer and businessman (1789-1859).

did.physdesc: 1 page of a bifolium. Quarto. Signed "H.G. Otis," Reserving pews in the church, one for "Mr. George Lyman, the other for a friend ..." Small hole to outer edge affecting one word; small portion of blank second leaf lacking. With "G. Sullivan Esq." to address panel.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Harrison Gray Otis (1765-1848) was a prominent Bostonian appointed by George Washington as U.S. Attorney for Massachusetts in 1796.

did.physdesc: 1/2 page. Octavo. In the hand of Elbridge Gerry, treasurer of the Corporation of St. Paul's Church Boston. A request to pay Mr. Huntington for services rendered by him and his family in the Choir at St. Paul's Church. Two lines of text to verso dated October 25, 1820 crossed out.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: The "Elbridge Gerry" referred to is possibly Elbridge Gerry Jr. (1793-1876), son of the American Founding Father Elbridge Gerry (1744-1814).

did.physdesc: "For 3 months hire of an organ ... recd. Payt. for my Father signed "G. Graupner"

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: G. Graupner is likely the son of noted musician, composer, and important early American music publisher Johann Christian Gottlieb Graupner (1767-1836)

did.unittitle: Manuscript receipt issued by Francis Wilby Esq.
did.physdesc: 213 x 142 mm. Lower edge ragged.

Issued by Francis Wilby Esq. to C. Nolen Jr. for "sundries for use of the choir of St. Paul's Church ... sewing... wood ... repairing benches... candlesticks, lamps and oil ... fruckage of benches... sewing manuscripts... music paper ... copying Sanctum Sanctorum chant; copying 2 Choruses from Kent's Anthems." Dated Boston, Febr. 12th 1822,

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Wilby, Boston auctioneer and owner of a "Fancy Shop" dealing in earthenware made by John and William Ridgway of England; John Ridgeway reported (in his "Beauties of America") a visit to St. Paul's in Boston on October 27, 1822, in the company of Francis Wilby, to hear a sermon by Dr. Jarvis.

did.physdesc: Receipt for $67.81 for services rendered by Francis Wilby "for two months and one days salary as organist from May 6th to July 7th 1822" signed G.K. Jackson. With remnants of red sealing wax.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Dr. George K. Jackson (1745-1822), a prominent English/American composer and organist, was the first organist at St. Paul's.

did.physdesc: Check in the amount of $23 issued by the Boston Bank, completed in manuscript, payable to Wilby and signed by Goodrich.

1 page. Quarto. To Wm. Goodrich. With integral address panel. Document recording a vote at a meeting of the Vestry of St. Paul's Church on August 3, 1826 authorizing the treasurer to advance William M. Goodrich $100 for organ building at St. Paul's and to repay Henry Codman $50 advanced to Goodrich, signed by Codman. With two separate receipts signed by Goodrich dated September 6, 1826 and September 7, 1826 confirming receipt of two $50 payments, Slightly browned; several small splits at folds; small tears with minor loss.
Receipt with "St. Pauls Church" at head. Dated Boston, Feb. 19th 1838 and signed by noted American organist and composer Ch[arles] Zeuner (1795-1857). 150 x 80 mm.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Charles Zeuner, organist at St. Paul's, published some of the earliest of collections of organ music in America (perhaps the first).

did.physdesc: 1 page of a bifolium. Octavo. Signed "J.C. Warren." To Henry Codman. Relative to adding pipes to the organ. Remnants of seal with corresponding tear to blank leaf.

1 page of a bifolium. Quarto. Addressed to Wm. Shummer, Bodoin Square. Greatorex agrees to "furnish the music at your church for the sum you mentioned" and accepts the position of organist: "I will engage to play the organ, to find an efficient choir to furnish books for its use, and to pay all the expenses ... for $1000 per ann.," requesting additional compensation "for funerals and other unusual services."With dark red seal intact; small area opposite seal lacking.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: H.W. Greatorex was a noted English-born American organist and composer (1813-1858).

did.unittitle: List of St. Paul's congregation members
did.physdesc: In a single hand. 2 pp. of a bifolium. Quarto.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical

did.physdesc: Addressed to Codman. 1 page of a bifolium. Octavo. With integral address panel. Remnants of seal.
Etten, Edwin J. van. The Cathedral Church of St. Paul Boston, The Story of the Music. 4 pp.
Program for organ performances at the Cathedral, Summer 1953, 4 pp.
